evidence_quotes.quote: the practice of publishers and developers shutting their games down without any upside to the consumers.

paragraphs.text: Hello wonderful people of the Digital Fairness Act (Or you who reads my words) The work that you guys have put into formulating the (DFA) is astoundingly important in the fight against shady or directly malicious practices in the sphere of video-games. But as important as the 5 points written in (DFA) are - there is a key issue that desperately need some attention. These 5 points are in danger of being a null/void - when we take into account that games are now facing being unplayable, and vanish into the history books, together with all the money that was put into them from the consumers. Now, naturally - Company's that are putting effort, money and serious work into making games should be rewarded monetarily if they make a great product, as on the flip side, consumers should be able to keep the product that they have paid for. The issue that is being highlighted from the (Stop Destroying Videogames (ECI)) is the practice of publishers and developers shutting their games down without any upside to the consumers. This practice has now been practiced for so long that it's becoming industry standard by this point. Problem is; there is no reason for the developers and publishers to not hand over some sort of server binary when they are sunsetting their game.. Well... Other than they would not be able to force or push people to their "next" game and do the whole rodeo all over again. As a long time video game player with 19 years active on steam and a library of nearly 2000 games and a huge pile of money put into this hobby of mine - this is important for me. In fact, equally as important as the 5 points presented in the (DFA). As we see with a lot of the ToS' of these games, if it was legal to just take the money and run, they would do that in a heart beat. My personal agenda is obvious, I want to be able to keep the art that I have legitimately purchased and not just loose all the products that I have spent almost 50-60k euros on all together (according to a website that calculates the total value of my steam-account). So in closing; please do consider looking into the Stop Destroying Videogames initiative and add a 6'th point to the list of practices that should be stopped. Thank you so much for reading - have a rest of your whole life <3 Kennie Nielsen, Denmark.
